common_name: Oca (Andean Wood Sorrel, New Zealand Yam)

planting.note: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep after last frost in cool soil; oca is photoperiod-sensitive and only initiates tuber formation once days shorten below about 12 hours in late summer or fall — in long-summer climates, use season-extension techniques to hasten tuberization or choose early-bulking varieties such as 'New Zealand Red'.

directions: Plant seed tubers 3–4 inches deep in loose, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; oca is the second most important Andean root crop after potato, cultivated for thousands of years across the Andes from Colombia to Argentina at elevations of 7,000–13,000 feet, where its tangy, lemony tubers are boiled, roasted, freeze-dried into 'caya', or left in the sun for several days to mellow the oxalic acid and develop sweetness. Tubers come in striking colors — white, yellow, orange, pink, red, and near-black — and range from pleasantly tart to mild and starchy depending on cultivar and post-harvest sun exposure. Harvest after the first light frost kills the foliage, which signals peak tuber development; in frost-free zones, cut back the vines in November to trigger dormancy.
sources: Lost Crops of the Incas — National Academies Press

planting.note: Plant small whole tubers or large tuber sections 3–4 inches deep in cool, moist soil after last frost; ulluco is highly photoperiod-sensitive and, like oca, will not form tubers until autumn day lengths shorten — it performs best where summers are cool (below 75°F) and the frost-free period extends into November.

directions: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in loose, moist, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; ulluco is a creeping, succulent-stemmed Andean tuber crop in the family Basellaceae, domesticated in the Andes over 5,000 years ago and second only to potato in importance across Bolivia, Peru, Ecuador, and Colombia where it is called 'papa lisa' (smooth potato). The tubers are waxy, colorful (yellow, orange, pink, red, purple), and retain their shape when cooked, making them excellent for stews such as the Bolivian dish 'soltero de ulluco'. Unlike potato, ulluco has never suffered a catastrophic blight and is rich in protein, calcium, and carotenoids. Harvest after first light frost kills back the vines; plants are almost entirely propagated vegetatively as fertile seed is rare.
sources: Lost Crops of the Incas — National Academies Press

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 4–6 inches deep in warm soil after last frost; in zones 8–9 mulch heavily after frost kills foliage and rhizomes will resprout from the ground — in zones 10–11 plants are evergreen perennials; start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in cold climates for a longer harvest window.

directions: Plant rhizome sections with at least one bud 4–6 inches deep in deep, rich, consistently moist to wet soil in full sun; achira (edible canna) is one of the oldest cultivated plants in the Americas, documented in Andean archaeological sites dating to 4,500 BC, where its large starchy rhizomes were eaten roasted, boiled, or dried and ground into a fine starch used in traditional biscuits and 'biscochos' throughout Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, and Bolivia. The starch granules are among the largest in the plant kingdom and produce an exceptionally clear, smooth gel prized in food processing. Tall, ornamental plants with broad paddle-like foliage and red or orange flowers are grown for both food and landscape value; in commercial production in Queensland and Vietnam, achira yields 20–40 tons of rhizome per acre. Harvest rhizomes at 9–12 months when leaves yellow; replant divisions for the following season.
sources: Lost Crops of the Incas — National Academies Press

common_name: Purple Yam (Ube, Water Yam, Winged Yam)

planting.note: Plant tuber sections (3–4 oz, each with at least one eye) 4–6 inches deep after last frost when soil has warmed to 70°F; provide a sturdy trellis or fence at least 6 feet tall — the vigorous vines twine counterclockwise and can reach 15–20 feet; aerial bulbils produced in the leaf axils can also be planted and will produce harvestable tubers in their second season.

directions: Plant tuber sections in loose, deep (18–24 inches), well-drained, fertile soil in full sun with strong trellis support; purple yam (ube) is the most widely cultivated yam species in the world and a treasured ingredient in Filipino cuisine, where its vivid violet-purple flesh and sweet, mildly nutty flavor are used in ube halaya (jam), halo-halo, ice cream, pastries, and a growing global trend in purple foods. Originating in Southeast Asia and now grown pantropically, D. alata produces large (up to 20 lb), winged-stemmed vines that require a full 9–12 month season for maximum tuber development; in frost-prone zones grow in the longest available frost-free window and harvest after vine dieback. The distinctive purple color comes from anthocyanins and deepens with cooking; unlike taro, the flesh is not irritating raw. Tubers store well for several months in cool, dry conditions.
sources: Philippine Root Crop Research and Training Center (PhilRootcrops)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in warm, moist soil after all frost danger has passed; arrowroot naturally grows beneath forest canopy and thrives in partial shade — a valuable trait for understory food gardens; in zones 9–11 it is an evergreen perennial, in zone 8 mulch rhizomes heavily after frost dieback for winter protection.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions 2–3 inches deep in rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; arrowroot is a tropical American perennial producing slender, finger-like rhizomes rich in a pure, easily digestible starch that has been cultivated in the Caribbean and tropical Americas for over 7,000 years — archaeological evidence from St. Vincent dates its cultivation to 5,000 BC. The starch, processed by washing, grating, and straining the rhizomes and drying the residue, produces a fine, neutral-flavored thickener superior to cornstarch for clear sauces and gravies because it gels at lower temperatures, does not impart a starchy flavor, and remains clear rather than cloudy. Plants prefer a warm, humid, partially shaded position and consistent moisture; harvest rhizomes at 10–12 months when foliage begins to yellow, retaining small tip sections to replant immediately for the following season.
sources: Lost Crops of the Incas — National Academies Press
